questions.question: Prove the correctness of Insertion Sort using loop invariant method. State and prove the three properties required for a loop invariant.
keyPoints: Initialization: Before first iteration (i=1), subarray A[0..0] contains single element - trivially sorted
Maintenance: At iteration i, assuming A[0..i-1] is sorted, we insert A[i] at correct position maintaining sorted order of A[0..i]
Termination: After loop ends (i=n), subarray A[0..n-1] contains all n elements in sorted order by the invariant

questions.question: Derive the time complexity of Insertion Sort for the best case, worst case, and average case. Show all calculations.
keyPoints: Best case: Already sorted - outer loop runs n-1 times, inner loop exits immediately after 1 comparison each = n-1 comparisons = O(n)
Worst case: Reverse sorted - for i=1 to n-1, inner loop runs i times = Σi=1 to n-1 = n(n-1)/2 = Θ(n²)
Average case: For random permutation, expected inner loop iterations = i/2, giving Σi/2 = n(n-1)/4 = Θ(n²)

questions.question: Trace Insertion Sort step-by-step on the array A = [31, 41, 59, 26, 41, 58]. Show the array state after each iteration of the outer loop.
keyPoints: i=2: key=59, compare with 41,31→no shifts. A=[31,41,59,26,41,58]

questions.question: Explain why Insertion Sort is considered stable. Under what condition would it become unstable?
keyPoints: Stability: Equal elements never pass each other because condition is arr[j] > key (strictly greater), not >=
When key equals arr[j], the while loop terminates, so equal elements stay in original relative order
If changed to arr[j] >= key, algorithm becomes unstable as key would be inserted before equal elements

questions.question: Why is Insertion Sort preferred over O(n log n) algorithms for small or nearly-sorted arrays? Provide analytical justification.
keyPoints: For sorted input: O(n) vs O(n log n) - Insertion Sort wins by factor of log n
For nearly-sorted (k inversions): O(n+k) vs O(n log n) - Insertion Sort wins when k << n log n
Constant factors smaller in Insertion Sort: no recursive overhead, cache-friendly sequential memory access
Hybrid algorithms (Timsort) use Insertion Sort for subarrays of size 32-64

questions.question: Derive the space complexity of Insertion Sort and explain why it is classified as an in-place sorting algorithm.
keyPoints: Space complexity: O(1) auxiliary space (excluding input array)
Uses only one temporary variable 'key' for swapping, plus loop counters i and j
All sorting done within original array - no additional arrays needed for merging or recursion
In-place means algorithm uses ≤ O(1) extra space beyond input

questions.question: Compare Insertion Sort with Selection Sort and Bubble Sort in terms of time complexity, stability, and adaptive behavior.
keyPoints: Time: All three have O(n²) worst/average case, but Insertion Sort has O(n) best case vs Selection/Bubble always O(n²)
Stability: Insertion Sort stable, Selection Sort unstable (swaps), Bubble Sort stable with flag optimization
Adaptiveness: Insertion Sort adaptive (O(n+k) for k inversions), Bubble Sort can be made adaptive, Selection Sort not adaptive
Swaps: Selection Sort exactly n-1 swaps, Insertion Sort up to n²/2 shifts, Bubble Sort variable

questions.question: Consider a modified Insertion Sort that uses binary search to find the insertion position. (a) What is the time complexity? Justify. (b) Why does this not improve asymptotic complexity? (c) Under what practical conditions might this modification still be beneficial?
keyPoints: (a) O(n²): Binary search takes O(log n) comparisons per element but shifting takes O(n) moves per element - dominant term remains O(n²)
(b) Asymptotically unchanged because shifting/insertion is O(n) per element, which dominates O(log n) comparisons; the inner loop work is still Θ(n) per iteration
(c) Beneficial when: comparison cost is high relative to move cost, elements are large records where moves are expensive, or cache performance matters due to better memory access patterns

questions.question: (a) Prove that Insertion Sort makes exactly n(n-1)/2 comparisons in the worst case. (b) If an array has exactly m inversions, prove that Insertion Sort performs exactly n-1+m comparisons. (c) What is the maximum number of inversions possible for n elements?
keyPoints: (a) Worst case: array in reverse order. For i from 1 to n-1, inner loop runs i times: Σi=1 to n-1 = n(n-1)/2 = Θ(n²)
(b) Each inversion requires one comparison in inner loop. Total comparisons = (n-1) from outer loop iterations + m from resolving inversions = n-1+m
(c) Maximum inversions = n(n-1)/2 for reverse-sorted array. Number of possible inversions equals number of pairs where i<j and A[i]>A[j]

questions.question: Design a recursive version of Insertion Sort and derive its time and space complexity. Also prove its correctness by stating the appropriate induction hypothesis.
keyPoints: Recursive algorithm: Sort(A, n) sorts first n-1 elements recursively, then inserts A[n-1] into sorted A[0..n-2] using insertion
Time: T(n) = T(n-1) + O(n) = O(n²) by unfolding the recurrence
Space: O(n) due to recursion stack depth (n function calls active simultaneously)

Induction hypothesis: Assume Sort(A, n-1) correctly sorts first n-1 elements. Base case: n=1 trivially sorted. Inductive step: after recursive call, first n-1 elements sorted; inserting nth element at correct position (by algorithm) maintains sorted order of all n elements
